section.title: Prologue
section.sectionRationale: This is the book's own self-introduction, a tightly built unit before John's first vision begins in verse 9: the title and chain of transmission, the first of the book's seven beatitudes, an epistolary greeting to seven real churches, a doxology to the one who loves and frees them, and the keynote oracle of Christ's coming, sealed by God's own self-declaration as the Alpha and the Omega.
section.thesis: Revelation announces itself before it shows anything: a disclosure of Jesus Christ, passed from God through an angel to John, promising blessing to those who read, hear, and keep it, and centered on the certainty that the crucified, reigning Christ is coming with the clouds, for he, with God the Almighty, holds the beginning and the end of history.
section.frameNote: Three units: the revelation and its first beatitude (1-3); the greeting to the seven churches and the doxology to the one who freed us (4-6); the coming oracle and the divine self-declaration, Alpha and Omega (7-8).

units.label: The revelation and its blessing
units.note: what must soon come to pass

verses.text: This is the {{apokalypsis|revelation}} of Jesus Christ, which God gave Him to show His servants what must soon come to pass. He made it known by sending His angel to His servant John,
who testifies to everything he saw. This is the word of God and the {{martyria|testimony}} of Jesus Christ.
{{makarios|Blessed}} is the one who reads aloud the words of this prophecy, and blessed are those who hear and obey what is written in it, because the time is near.

units.label: Greeting and doxology
units.note: grace, peace, and praise to the one who freed us

verses.text: John, To the seven churches in the province of Asia: Grace and peace to you from Him who is and was and is to come, and from the seven Spirits before His throne,
and from Jesus Christ, the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth. To Him who loves us and has released us from our sins by His blood,
who has made us to be a {{basileia|kingdom}}, priests to His God and Father, to Him be the glory and power forever and ever! Amen.

units.label: The coming oracle and the Almighty's self-declaration
units.note: he is coming with the clouds

verses.text: Behold, He is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see Him, even those who pierced Him. And all the tribes of the earth will mourn because of Him. So shall it be! Amen.
“I am the {{alpha_omega|Alpha and the Omega}},” says the Lord God, who is and was and is to come, the {{pantokrator|Almighty}}.

apokalypsis.gloss: An unveiling, uncovering, disclosure of what was hidden. The book takes its name from this very word: it exists not to conceal but to reveal the risen Christ and what must soon come to pass, for the strengthening of his servants.

martyria.gloss: Testimony, witness, borne in something like a legal or eyewitness sense. John's book is his testified witness of what he saw, joined to the testimony Jesus himself bears; the word is the root of the English martyr, one who bears such testimony even at the cost of life.

makarios.gloss: Blessed, happy, to be congratulated, the word of the Beatitudes. This is the first of seven beatitudes scattered through Revelation, pronounced here on those who read the prophecy aloud, hear it, and keep what is written in it.

basileia.gloss: Kingdom, royal rule. Christ has made those he has freed to be a kingdom, priests to his God and Father, echoing Exodus 19:6's calling of Israel as a kingdom of priests, now given to the church redeemed by his blood.

alpha_omega.gloss: The first and last letters of the Greek alphabet, a title of absolute sovereignty over history's whole span, claimed here by the Lord God and later, in Revelation 22:13, claimed by the risen Christ himself.

pantokrator.gloss: The Almighty, the one who holds and rules all things. A favorite divine title in Revelation, it grounds every terror the book will describe in the unshaken sovereignty of the God who is, and was, and is to come.

basic.context: Revelation opens by telling us what it is before it shows us anything: a disclosure of Jesus Christ, given by God, passed through an angel, to John, for the churches. John pronounces the first of the book's blessings on anyone who reads, hears, and keeps this prophecy. Then, like a letter, he greets seven real churches in Asia, breaks into praise of Christ who loves and frees them, and closes the prologue with the announcement that Christ is coming with the clouds and that God calls himself the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end.
basic.hermeneutics: Christians disagree over how quickly 'what must soon come to pass' and 'the time is near' should be fulfilled, since nearly two thousand years have passed. Some stress that these words were urgently true for the first readers; others read 'soon' as apocalyptic language for certainty rather than a countdown, since with the Lord a thousand years are as a day. Whole schools of reading Revelation, preterist, historicist, futurist, and idealist, differ over how the book's visions map onto history, but the prologue itself asks something simpler first: read it, hear it, keep it.
basic.originalLanguages: The book's very name is apokalypsis, an unveiling. John's testimony, martyria, joins the testimony of Jesus himself. The first beatitude, makarios, blesses those who keep this prophecy. Christ has made believers a basileia, a kingdom, of priests. And twice in these eight verses God is named with titles of total sovereignty: the Alpha and the Omega, and the Pantokrator, the Almighty.
basic.typology: The kingdom of priests recalls Exodus 19:6, Israel's calling at Sinai, now fulfilled in Christ's freed people. The Alpha and Omega title echoes Isaiah's repeated 'I am the first and I am the last' for the LORD, fulfilled when the risen Christ claims the very same title of himself in Revelation 22:13. And 'coming with the clouds' draws directly on Daniel 7:13's Son of Man, fulfilled in Christ's promised return.
basic.christ: Jesus is named the faithful witness, the firstborn from the dead, and the ruler of the kings of the earth, three titles that together announce his death, resurrection, and coming universal reign. He has already loved us and freed us from our sins by his blood, and made us a kingdom and priests, before the book shows a single vision. Everything that follows in Revelation unfolds from this Christ, already victorious, already loved, already coming again.
basic.god: God is named by the sweeping, timeless formula 'who is and was and is to come,' and finally as the Almighty, the Pantokrator. Whatever fearsome things the book will unveil, they occur under the sovereignty of this God, who holds all things, and whose Spirit, sevenfold before the throne, is present with the churches from the first verse.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 19:6 calls Israel a kingdom of priests. Isaiah 44:6 has the LORD say 'I am the first and I am the last.' Daniel 7:13 pictures one like a son of man coming with the clouds of heaven. Revelation 22:13 has the risen Christ claim the Alpha and Omega title for himself.
basic.oneStory: Before a single vision unfolds, Revelation tells us plainly what kind of book this is and who stands at its center: the crucified and risen Christ, already loving and freeing his people, already ruling the kings of the earth, and certainly coming again. The whole sweep of Scripture, creation, fall, promise, exodus, kingdom, exile, and gospel, arrives here at its final chapter's opening line: blessed are those who keep what is written, because the one who is, and was, and is to come, holds the end as surely as he held the beginning.

context.literary: A self-contained prologue combining three genres at once: apocalypse (v.1, an unveiling), prophecy (v.3, a prophecy to be read aloud), and letter (vv.4-6, a greeting to named churches). This threefold identity shapes how the whole book should be read: as a Spirit-given disclosure, meant to be heard in worship, addressed to real congregations under real pressure.
context.historical: Written to seven churches in the Roman province of Asia, cities under the pressures of emperor cult, trade guild idolatry, and social exclusion for refusing to honor Rome's gods. The prologue's opening claim, that Jesus Christ is the ruler of the kings of the earth, is a direct counter-claim to Caesar's own propaganda.
context.canonical: Revelation is the last book of the Christian canon, and its prologue signals that it intends to bring the whole biblical story to its close: the kingdom of priests promised at Sinai, the coming Son of Man from Daniel, and the divine self-naming of Isaiah's LORD, all converge here on Jesus Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To identify this book as a genuine disclosure of Jesus Christ, given through a traceable chain of transmission from God to Christ to angel to John to the churches, so that hearing and keeping it carries real authority, and to set the whole book's tone with praise of the crucified, reigning Christ and the certainty of his coming.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The prologue describes what this book is and does, revelation, testimony, prophecy, blessing; it does not prescribe a detailed timetable for future events. Its abiding call is to read, hear, and keep what is written, trusting the one who is, and was, and is to come.

debates.question: What does it mean that 'what must soon come to pass' (v.1) and 'the time is near' (v.3), given that nearly two thousand years have now passed?

views.label: Genuinely imminent for the first readers
views.case: Preterist and near-term readers stress that these words carried real urgency for the seven churches then facing pressure and persecution, and that much of Revelation's early material found substantial fulfillment in events close to their own time.

views.label: Certain rather than scheduled
views.case: Futurist and idealist readers note that apocalyptic language regularly uses 'soon' and 'near' to express the certainty and nearness of God's purposes rather than a strict human timetable, since with the Lord a day is as a thousand years, so the same words can rightly describe events still future to us.

debates.bearing: However the timing is read, every school agrees the purpose is the same: to press urgent, wide-awake obedience on the churches now, not to fuel speculative date-setting calculations.

debates.question: How should the four major traditions for reading Revelation, preterist, historicist, futurist, and idealist, be held as the book begins?

views.label: The book primarily addresses its own first century
views.case: Preterist readers see most of Revelation's judgments fulfilled substantially in the fall of Jerusalem or the pressures of Roman persecution under emperors like Domitian, close to John's own day.

views.label: The book maps church history, or describes the very end, or pictures recurring realities
views.case: Historicist readers trace the visions across the centuries of church history; futurist readers see most of the book, especially from chapter 4 onward, describing events still to come at history's close; idealist readers see the symbols picturing the recurring conflict between Christ's church and the world in every age.

debates.bearing: Faithful interpreters across these traditions often blend elements of more than one; the prologue itself puts the weight not on solving this scheme first but on reading, hearing, and keeping the prophecy now, whatever its precise unfolding proves to be.

study.originalLanguages: The book names itself an apokalypsis, an unveiling, and John's own martyria, testimony, is bound to the testimony of Jesus. The first of the book's seven beatitudes calls its hearers makarios, blessed, for keeping what is written. Christ has already made his freed people a basileia, a kingdom, of priests. And the prologue closes with two overlapping titles of total sovereignty: the Alpha and the Omega, spanning the Greek alphabet's whole span, and the Pantokrator, the Almighty, the one who holds and rules all things, however fearsome the visions to come.

typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the church, freed by Christ's blood and made a kingdom and priests to God and Father.

typology.type: I am the first and I am the last, Isaiah 44:6
typology.fulfillment: Claimed here by God and claimed again by the risen Christ himself in Revelation 22:13, the Alpha and the Omega.

typology.type: One like a son of man coming with the clouds of heaven, Daniel 7:13
typology.fulfillment: The oracle of verse 7, Christ coming with the clouds, seen by every eye, including those who pierced him.

study.christ: In four short verses Christ is named the faithful witness, who did not waver even to death; the firstborn from the dead, the first of a great resurrection harvest; and the ruler of the kings of the earth, already reigning over every throne, however it may look otherwise. He has already loved his people and released them from their sins by his blood, already made them a kingdom and priests. And the prologue's climactic oracle, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him, even those who pierced him, identifies him with the Alpha and Omega, the eternal God himself.
study.god: God is named with a title stretched across all of time, who is and was and is to come, and, at the prologue's close, the Almighty, the Pantokrator, who rules all things absolutely. This is the God under whose sovereignty every terror in the pages ahead will unfold, and whose sevenfold Spirit stands before his throne, present with his churches from the very first greeting.

crossReferences.note: Israel called a kingdom of priests, now fulfilled in the church freed by Christ's blood.
The LORD says, 'I am the first and I am the last,' the root of the Alpha and Omega title.
One like a son of man coming with the clouds of heaven, echoed in the coming oracle of verse 7.
The risen Christ claims the Alpha and Omega title for himself at the book's close, framing the whole book.

study.oneStory: Revelation opens by naming itself: an unveiling of Jesus Christ, passed down a chain of trust to reach ordinary churches under real pressure, promising blessing to those who read, hear, and keep it. Its first beatitude, its greeting, and its doxology all converge on one figure, the faithful witness, firstborn from the dead, ruler of earth's kings, who is coming with the clouds and who, with God the Almighty, holds the Alpha and the Omega of the whole story the Bible has been telling since Genesis one.

sermon.title: Revelation (Part 2)
sermon.why: Carson works closely through Revelation 1:4-8, the greeting, the doxology to Christ, and the coming oracle at the heart of this prologue, drawing out the sovereignty of God and the certainty of Christ's return.

quiz.q: What does the word 'revelation' (apokalypsis) tell us about the purpose of this book?

options.text: It exists to unveil and disclose Jesus Christ and what must soon come to pass, not to conceal him.
options.note: The book's very name announces disclosure, not obscurity or secrecy.

options.text: It is a coded book that only specialists can ever understand.
options.note: The imagery is often symbolic, but the book calls itself an unveiling, meant to be read aloud and understood.

options.text: It is mainly a collection of predictions with no connection to Jesus Christ.
options.note: It is explicitly the revelation of Jesus Christ, centered on his person.

options.text: It is meant to remain permanently hidden from the church.
options.note: The opposite; it is meant to be read, heard, and kept, verse 3.

quiz.q: Who does the first beatitude of Revelation (v.3) bless?

options.text: Those who read the prophecy aloud, hear it, and keep what is written in it.
options.note: Verse 3 names all three: reading, hearing, and keeping.

options.text: Only those who correctly predict the book's future events.
options.note: The blessing is for obedient hearing and keeping, not for successful prediction.

options.text: Only the apostle John himself.
options.note: The blessing extends to readers, hearers, and keepers generally, not to John alone.

options.text: No one; the blessing is only for a future generation.
options.note: The blessing is pronounced because the time is near, addressed to the churches then and now.

quiz.q: What has Christ already done for believers, according to the doxology in verses 5-6?

options.text: Loved them and released them from their sins by his blood, and made them a kingdom and priests.
options.note: Verses 5-6 state this directly as an accomplished doxology.

options.text: Promised to love them only after they overcome every trial in the book.
options.note: The love and freeing are already accomplished, not conditioned on surviving the book's trials.

options.text: Made them earthly kings with political power over Rome.
options.note: The kingdom given is priestly and spiritual, not a claim to worldly political rule.

options.text: Left them to earn their own release from sin.
options.note: The text says Christ released them by his blood, a finished work, not a wage they earned.

quiz.q: What is the content of the coming oracle in verse 7?

options.text: Christ is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him, including those who pierced him, and all tribes will mourn.
options.note: This is the direct content of the oracle, echoing Daniel 7:13 and Zechariah 12:10.

options.text: Christ will return invisibly and only a few will notice.
options.note: The opposite; every eye will see him, a public, universal event.

options.text: Only the seven churches in Asia will witness Christ's coming.
options.note: The oracle says every eye and all the tribes of the earth, a universal scope.

options.text: It describes a past event already completed in the first century.
options.note: It is framed as a still-future coming oracle, sealed with 'so shall it be, Amen.'

quiz.q: What does the title 'Alpha and the Omega' claim?

options.text: Total sovereignty over the whole span of history, from beginning to end.
options.note: The first and last letters of the Greek alphabet picture complete, sovereign scope.

options.text: That God only cares about the beginning of history, not its end.
options.note: The title spans both ends together, beginning and end alike.

options.text: A minor literary flourish with no real theological weight.
options.note: It is a weighty divine self-declaration, echoed by Christ himself in Revelation 22:13.

options.text: A title used only once in Revelation and never repeated.
options.note: The risen Christ claims the same title again in Revelation 22:13.

questions: The first beatitude blesses those who read, hear, and keep this prophecy. Which of those three, reading, hearing, or keeping, do you find hardest, and why?
Christ has already loved you and freed you before you did anything in response. How does resting in that finished doxology change the way you face whatever this book will go on to describe?
'The time is near' has felt urgent to believers for two thousand years. How do you hold both patience and urgency together as you wait for Christ's coming?
Every eye will see him, even those who pierced him. What does it mean to you that Christ's return will be unmistakably public, seen and known by all, rather than private or hidden?
God calls himself the Alpha and the Omega, the beginning and the end. Where in your life right now do you most need to remember that he holds both ends of your story?

translationNotes.note: 'Soon' can also be rendered suddenly or quickly, stressing the swift, decisive nature of the events once they begin rather than only their nearness in time.
'The province of Asia' is literally just 'Asia' in the Greek, referring to the Roman province in what is now western Turkey, not the continent.
'The seven Spirits before His throne' can also be rendered the sevenfold Spirit, understood as a description of the fullness of the one Holy Spirit rather than seven separate spirits.
Some later manuscripts (the Textus Receptus) read 'the Alpha and the Omega, the Beginning and the End,' adding a second, explanatory pair of titles to the same claim.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the project's makor-scripture toolkit for Revelation 1:1-8. Em dashes in verses 6, 7, and the stray spacing in verse 8's quotation mark were corrected to commas and clean punctuation. Sermon link for D. A. Carson's 'Revelation (Part 2)' verified live this session on thegospelcoalition.org; it covers verses 4-8 of this section directly.
